title: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
author: John Taylor
orientation: An open letter from a seventy-something former pro-lifer to abolitionists ahead of the 'Repent With Us' Abolition Now Conference, urging the movement to model lifelong, humble repentance rather than wield it as a self-righteous cudgel against pro-lifers. Taylor draws on Ezra's example, identifies pride, intellectual laziness, and spiritual blindness as impediments, and warns that abolitionists themselves must continually repent if they hope to lead others.
audiences: Christian newly engaged in cultural issues

questions.question: Why does Taylor say repentance cannot be treated as a one-time event tied to conversion?
questions.audience: Christian newly engaged in cultural issues
questions.answer: Taylor insists that Christ willed the entire life of believers to be one of repentance, so claiming 'I already repented when I was saved' misunderstands the doctrine. Conversion is only the first instance; the believer's mind must be continually renewed against the world's thought patterns until the very end of life.
questions.quote: So a Christian who resists the Abolitionist's call to repentance saying "I already repented when I was saved" does not understand repentance well. Repenting from unbelief to belief in Christ is only the first instance of repentance.

questions.question: How does Taylor warn abolitionists against treating their cause as a mark of spiritual superiority?
questions.answer: He warns that it is dangerously easy to silently regard abolitionism as a 'spiritual merit badge' that elevates abolitionists above other Christians. The honest difference, he argues, is not personal virtue but the order in which God opened eyes — a gift, not an achievement.
questions.quote: Let us not think of Abolitionism as a spiritual merit badge that makes us better than other Christians. (I know, we won't *say* that, but how easy it is to *think* that.) The fundamental difference between me and a pro-lifer is that God opened my eyes first.

questions.question: What does Taylor identify as the risk when abolitionists call others to repentance without modeling it themselves?
questions.answer: He compares it to wannabe evangelists whose self-righteous presentation guarantees rejection — and who then blame the listener's 'hardness of heart' rather than examining their own approach. Abolitionists who fail to lead by example will come across as obnoxious and shut down the very people they hope to win.
questions.quote: If we are not leading by example in continued repentance, won't we come across as obnoxious? I suspect most of us have winced to see wannabe evangelists guarantee their hearers will reject their self-righteous Gospel presentation.

questions.question: Why does Taylor argue that intellectual laziness — not lack of zeal — is a primary impediment for many pro-lifers?
questions.answer: Taylor confesses this was his own primary obstacle: pro-lifers already believe Roe is immoral, but most have never done the work to see that Roe is constitutionally hollow and can simply be ignored by states. Without that intellectual labor, they default to the incrementalist party line of waiting on the Supreme Court.
questions.quote: I can't speak for all, but intellectual laziness was a key impediment for me as a pro-lifer. I don't ever expect to find a pro-lifer who does not believe that *Roe v. Wade* is immoral. They need to be *shown* that *Roe* is no "law of the land" but an emperor with no constitutional clothes.

questions.question: How does Taylor frame spiritual blindness as a shared condition rather than a pro-lifer problem?
questions.answer: He recalls being a Christian for at least fifteen years before God opened his eyes to a basic truth he had read in Scripture, and notes that Jesus healed more blind people in the Gospels than any other affliction. Abolitionists, he says, are still blind in places they cannot yet see, and impatience with pro-lifers' blindness only feeds pride.
questions.quote: My fellow abolitionists, not only were we once spiritually blind in many areas, we have no idea where we are *still* blind! Is there a reason the Gospels record Jesus healing more blind people than any other malady?

questions.question: What does Taylor name as the most stubborn impediment to repentance, and how does he say it operates on both sides?
questions.answer: Pride is the most stubborn impediment. Pro-life leaders refuse to acknowledge incompetence after 49 years and 63 million dead, but Taylor immediately turns the warning back on abolitionists by citing Christ's teaching on judgment and Paul's question about boasting in what one has merely received.
questions.quote: The most stubborn impediment to repentance, however, is pride.

questions.question: How does Ezra's prayer model the kind of repentance Taylor wants abolitionists to imitate?
questions.answer: Ezra prayed about the offenders' sins as if he himself were guilty — saying 'we' rather than 'they' — even though he had not committed those sins. His humility moved others to confess and act, and Taylor proposes that abolitionists who mourned with the same identification with pro-lifers might see God do similar work in their movement.
questions.quote: Notice that Ezra prays as if he was just as guilty as the offenders. He had not committed the sins they had, but he had his own, and confessed their sins as if he had joined in committing them, always saying "we", not "they".

questions.question: How does Taylor link the example of Jesus to the abolitionist's posture toward pro-lifers?
questions.audience: Christian newly engaged in cultural issues
questions.answer: Even Jesus, the only one who never needed repentance, submitted to John's baptism of repentance and identified Himself with sinners through the cross. Taylor argues that abolitionists who refuse to identify with the sins of pro-life brethren do not yet fully understand the Gospel they claim to preach.
questions.quote: If we do not identify with the sins of our brethren whose eyes God has not yet opened the way He graciously opened ours, how well do we understand the Gospel?

questions.question: What does Taylor's thief illustration teach about the shape of true repentance?
questions.audience: Christian newly engaged in cultural issues
questions.answer: Repentance is multi-layered and progressive: the converted thief must first stop stealing, then learn to support himself by labor (even if it means a serious income drop and learning a trade from scratch), and finally earn enough to give to others in need. Repentance is ongoing rather than a vintage label dated to a single moment.
questions.quote: Repentance is not like an old wine bottle: "Bottled in 2012." It is ongoing.

questions.question: What sharp warning does Taylor give pro-lifers about complicity in murder?
questions.answer: Citing the Apostle's blunt statement that no murderer has eternal life, Taylor says pro-lifers must stop being accessories to murder by funding pro-life organizations that oppose abolition, by pushing state laws that permit murder, and by telling post-abortive women they are not murderers — which prevents full confession and forgiveness.
questions.quote: They need to *stop* being accessories to murder by supporting pro-life organizations that oppose abolition, and by pushing State laws that allow murder. They must also *stop* telling post-abortive women that they are not murderers, for how can they be fully forgiven of their sin if they don't confess what it was?

questions.question: What danger does Taylor warn even committed abolitionists about regarding their own activism?
questions.answer: He cautions that dedication to an activist cause — even a righteous one — can quietly eclipse zeal for Christ Himself. Taylor admits he has had to repent of this and will likely have to again, because Christ tolerates no rivals regardless of how worthy the competing devotion may seem.
questions.quote: It is easy for dedication to an activist cause to eclipse zeal for Christ. I've had to repent of that, too, and I will do it again as an Abolitionist if I'm not careful. Christ tolerates no rivals, even if it is a good cause.

questions.question: Why does Taylor distinguish between the sharpness owed to pro-lifers and the sharpness owed to those clearly outside the kingdom?
questions.answer: Taylor notes that while New Testament epistles addressed church problems, the harshest letters were rare, focused on issues that would keep readers out of the kingdom, and were written by those who knew the readers personally. He has read many critiques of pro-lifers but no argument that being pro-life itself condemns one to hell, so the rebuke must be measured.
questions.quote: Nevertheless, the harsh epistles were few, they generally addressed issues that would keep readers out of the Kingdom, and the writer knew the readers personally. I have read many complaints about pro-lifers, but so far, no argumentation that being pro-life will drag one to hell.

related.title: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
related.reason: Companion piece from the same January 2022 'Repent With Us' conference push that argues incrementalist legislation actively obstructs the very repentance Taylor calls for.

related.title: Pastor Sam Ketcher: Repent With Us and Come to Abolition Day
related.reason: Another invitation tied to the Abolition Now / Repent With Us conference theme that frames the gathering Taylor was unable to attend.

related.title: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
related.reason: Concrete case study in pro-life leadership refusing the kind of humble, ongoing repentance Taylor models in his open letter.

related.title: Fruits of Abolitionism: Is True Repentance Necessary?
related.reason: Directly extends the conversation by asking whether genuine repentance is the indispensable fruit of abolitionism Taylor describes.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Sharpens the abolitionist/pro-life distinction Taylor explores, helping readers see why the call to 'repent with us' is not merely semantic.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Façade
related.reason: Illustrates the institutional pro-life failures Taylor cites as evidence that pride and incrementalism still need confronting at the leadership level.
